skill.name: github-project-contributor-finder-api-skill
skill.description: Find GitHub repositories and contributor profiles through the BrowserAct GitHub Project and Contributor Finder API. The skill searches by keywords, stars, update date, page count, and result limits, then returns repository metrics and public contributor contact links.
skill.summary: Find GitHub projects and public contributor contact details using BrowserAct workflow APIs.
skill.author: browser-act

security_audit.summary: The confirmed risk is a local Python script that sends a user-provided BrowserAct API key to BrowserAct workflow endpoints and retrieves public GitHub contributor data. The static code-execution, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks, examples, and ordinary status handling. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, but publication should warn users about third-party API use and credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Third-Party API Requests
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The script reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY and places it in an Authorization header for BrowserAct workflow API requests. This is required for the skill, but it exposes a user credential to a third-party service and should be disclosed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly reads an API key and sends authenticated requests to BrowserAct. The behavior matches the documented purpose, so the risk is credential handling rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Run Local Python Script
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs the agent to execute a Python script with user-provided search parameters. Arguments are passed to Python and JSON rather than interpolated into a shell command, which lowers injection risk but still requires script execution trust.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented invocation is explicit and the script parses CLI arguments. I did not find shell interpolation, but marketplace users should know that local code runs.

medium_findings.title: Public Contributor Contact Collection
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill is designed to collect public GitHub contributor profile links, websites, and Twitter handles. This supports legitimate recruiting and research, but users should apply outreach, privacy, and platform policy controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly lists public contributor contact fields and lead generation use cases. This is intentional functionality, not hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby or Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The repeated static alerts for Ruby or shell backtick execution point to Markdown formatting and documented command examples. No Ruby backtick operator or shell command construction was found in the cited skill instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited file is Markdown and the flagged tokens are inline code or fenced command examples. The actual executable file is Python and contains no shell backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Have No Supporting Code
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings cite prose, input descriptions, and output field names. No hashing, encryption, MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4 usage appears in the reviewed code at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows descriptions and examples, not cryptographic operations. The Python script also does not import or call crypto libraries.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Status and Error Handling
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reconnaissance findings correspond to string checks, task identifiers, and marketing claims about avoiding hallucinations. They do not inspect host files, network interfaces, processes, or system configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code handles API responses and prints task status. I found no commands or APIs that enumerate the local system.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documented Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hardcoded URLs point to BrowserAct API, console, and plan pages. They are expected for this integration and are not suspicious domains based on the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs align with the skill name, setup instructions, and API workflow. I did not find evidence of unrelated webhook, paste, or tracking endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Combined Script Execution, Network Access, and Credential Use
dangerous_patterns.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The static critical heuristic is contextually legitimate, but the combination is real. The agent runs a local Python script, reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY, and sends authenticated requests to BrowserAct endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The three behaviors are all present in the source. I did not find malicious intent, hidden destinations, or prompt injection, so this is medium risk rather than a block.

content.value_statement: Finding active GitHub projects and contributor contacts takes time across search results and profiles. This skill uses BrowserAct workflows to return repository metrics and public contributor links from focused search criteria.
actual_capabilities: Searches GitHub repositories by keyword, minimum stars, and last updated date.
Runs a BrowserAct workflow through a local Python script.
Polls task status until the workflow finishes or times out.
Returns repository names, URLs, owners, descriptions, and star counts.
Returns public contributor profile links, bios, websites, and Twitter handles.
Handles invalid API keys, concurrency limits, task failures, and one retry path.
limitations: Requires a valid BROWSERACT_API_KEY environment variable.
Sends search parameters and API authentication to BrowserAct services.
Results depend on BrowserAct workflow availability and GitHub public data.
Long-running workflows can take several minutes and may time out.

use_cases.title: Recruit Open-Source Developers
use_cases.description: Find contributors in active repositories and review public profiles before outreach.

use_cases.title: Map Developer Communities
use_cases.description: Identify active projects and contributors around a technology, framework, or tool category.

use_cases.title: Research Repository Ecosystems
use_cases.description: Collect repository metrics and public contributor signals for technology trend analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Repository Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Find GitHub repositories about browser automation with at least 100 stars. Return project names, URLs, owners, and contributors.

prompt_templates.title: Recent Framework Contributors
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for React framework repositories updated after 2026-01-01 with at least 1000 stars. Include public contributor websites when available.

prompt_templates.title: Focused Recruiting List
prompt_templates.prompt: Find machine learning repositories with at least 500 stars. Check two pages and return five repositories per page with contributor contact links.

prompt_templates.title: Competitive Ecosystem Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Research active browser testing projects updated after 2025-06-01. Compare repository metrics and identify contributors who may fit developer outreach.

output_examples.input: Search for browser automation repositories with at least 100 stars.
output: Repository list with names, GitHub URLs, owners, descriptions, and star counts.
Contributor profiles with GitHub profile URLs, bios, public websites, and Twitter handles when available.
Status updates while the BrowserAct task runs.

output_examples.input: Find React framework contributors from recently updated repositories.
output_examples.output: A concise research summary showing matching repositories, active maintainers, public profile details, and outreach-ready links.

output_examples.input: Build a developer relations lead list for machine learning tools.
output_examples.output: A prioritized list of relevant repositories and public contributors, with notes on repository activity and contact availability.

best_practices: Use narrow keywords and realistic star thresholds to keep results relevant.
Store BROWSERACT_API_KEY securely and avoid sharing it in prompts or logs.
Review public contact data for consent, relevance, and outreach policy compliance.
anti_patterns: Do not use broad searches that create noisy lead lists.
Do not treat public profile links as permission for bulk unsolicited outreach.
Do not retry invalid credentials or concurrency-limit errors repeatedly.

faq.question: What API key does this skill require?
faq.answer: It requires BROWSERACT_API_KEY for BrowserAct workflow access.

faq.question: Does it use the GitHub API directly?
faq.answer: No. The script calls BrowserAct workflow endpoints that perform the repository and contributor search.

faq.question: What search filters are supported?
faq.answer: It supports keywords, minimum stars, updated date, page turns, and result count per page.

faq.question: What contributor data can it return?
faq.answer: It can return public GitHub profile URLs, bios, repository summaries, personal websites, and Twitter handles.

faq.question: How long can a search take?
faq.answer: The script polls for up to 900 seconds and prints timestamped task status updates.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a warning because it runs a script and sends an API key to BrowserAct.
